segments.s585-g9: This clause states an identification and a possession using simple past 'بود' (was). Word order is poetic: 'دلارام' (proper name) is the predicate, followed by the pronoun 'او' (her/his) and the past copula 'بود' (was). The conjunction 'و' links the next nominal phrase 'هم کام اوی' where 'هم' adds 'also/indeed', 'کام' is an abstract noun 'desire/wish', and the enclitic possessive 'اوی' (= 'his/her') marks whose desire it is.
segments.s585-g10: This is a temporal/habitual statement with the adverb 'همیشه' (always) starting it. 'به لب داشتن نام' is an idiomatic construction meaning 'to have a name on (one's) lips' — 'به' is a prepositional particle introducing the locus ('on/at the lip'), 'لب' is the noun 'lip', and 'داشتی' is the second-person past 'you had/kept'. 'نام اوی' is the possessed noun 'her name' with the enclitic 'اوی'.
segments.s585-g11: 'به‌روز' is a temporal prepositional phrase 'on the day (of ...)', placed up front. 'شکارش' is the noun 'شکار' (hunt/prey) with the third-person enclitic 'ش' (= 'his/its'), and 'هیون' is the direct object 'beast/monster'. 'خواستی' is the second-person past 'you wanted/wished', so the whole segment reads 'On the hunting day you wished/desired the beast.'
segments.s585-g12: 'که' introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that' / 'that'). 'پشتش' = 'پشت' (back) + enclitic 'ش' (its/his). The phrase 'به دیبا' indicates the material/covering ('with/in brocade'), and 'بیاراستی' is a classical verbal form meaning 'you might adorn/arrange' (an optative/intentive past form used in epic diction). The clause therefore expresses the purpose of the desire: to deck/adorn its back with brocade.
segments.s585-g13: Poetic inverted word order: 'فروهشته' ('scattered') starts the line as a participial/predicative adjective, followed by the preposition 'ز' (poetic 'از' = 'from/of'), the coordinating 'و' (here linking elements in the poetic phrasing), the numeral 'چار' used as 'four', and the past copula 'بودی' ('were'). 'رکیب' is the noun 'companion/associate' (here 'companions' or 'riders'). Together they mean 'From him four companions were scattered' (i.e. four companions of his were dispersed).
segments.s585-g14: 'همی' is an archaic/poetic adverb indicating continuous or habitual action (roughly 'kept on/was always'), 'تاختی' is second-person past 'you galloped/charged', and the prepositional phrase 'در فراز و نشیب' ('in/through rises and descents') specifies the terrain or the alternation of high and low. The clause describes the addressed person continuously rushing about over ups and downs.
segments.s585-g15: 'رکابش' is 'رکاب' (stirrup) with the possessive enclitic 'ش' ('his'). The sequence 'دو زرین دو سیمین' enumerates two stirrups of gold and two of silver. The final word here 'بدی' is an archaic second‑person past form meaning 'you gave' or 'you made/placed' (classical epic usage differs from the modern homograph meaning 'evil'), so the clause says 'you gave/made his stirrups two golden, two silver.'
segments.s585-g16: 'همان' ('that/those/the same') points back to the stirrups; 'هر یکی' = 'each one'; 'گوهر' is 'gem/jewel' and 'آگین' is an adjective 'gem‑set / shining / jewel‑adorned.' The final 'بدی' again is the archaic 'you gave/you made' (past), so the line means 'to each of those you gave a gem‑adorned (finishing).' Grammatically, the clause repeats and specifies that each stirrup was furnished with jewels.
segments.s585-g65: This is an archaic/poetic counterfactual conditional: 'اگر' introduces the condition. The pattern 'اگر + [verb] + بودی' renders a past counterfactual ('if you had ...'). Here 'کند بودی' functions as the protasis ('if you had been able / if you had done'), and the following 'گشاد برم' is the apodosis in which 'برم' is a 1st-person jussive/conditional ('I would ...') derived from بردن. 'گشاد' (literally 'wide, gaping') qualifies the action that would have been done. Word order places the condition before the result, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s585-g66: 'ازین' = 'از + این' ('from/ because of this') introduces a source or cause. 'زخم' is the noun 'wound'. 'ننگی' is used predicatively as 'shameful / a disgrace', and 'شدی' is 2nd-person past 'you became'. 'گوهرم' is vocative/nominal address 'my jewel' (گوهر + possessive suffix -م). The clause thus states a consequence: 'From this wound you became shameful, my jewel.'
segments.s585-g67: 'چو' is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'when'. 'او' is the subject 'he'. The prepositional phrase 'زیر پای هیون' literally 'under the foot of the beast' marks location/position. 'در' functions as a locative/directional preposition 'into', and 'سپرد' is a past transitive verb 'he entrusted/placed/threw (into)'. The object of سپرد is omitted here (understood from context), which is common in epic narrative.
segments.s585-g68: 'به نخچیر' = 'as spoil / among the spoil/prey' (به + نخچیر). 'زان پس' is a compound temporal phrase 'after that' (زان = 'from that/thereof', پس = 'after'). 'کنیزک' is the object 'the little maid/maidservant' (diminutive). The final word 'نبرد' here functions as a past verbal form meaning 'he took' (contextually 'took as spoil'); however, the senses supplied in the word list do not match this verbal use, so treat the listed senses as inapplicable.
segments.s585-g17: Poetic word order: همان ('that/right there') sets the scene; زیر is the locative 'beneath'; ترک + ش is the compound written here as ترکِ + -ش (enclitic = 'his'), so ترکش = 'his quiver' (a possessive clitic attached to the noun). کمان مهره داشت is a compact clause 'the bow had a jewel' (کمان = subject, مهره = object/noun 'bead, gem', داشت = past-tense verb 'had'). Overall this segment places a small jeweled ornament under his quiver/bow.
segments.s585-g18: دلاور is the subject 'the valiant one / hero'. ز is the poetic preposition corresponding to modern از ('from'/'of') introducing the source/extent of what follows. هر دانشی بهره داشت literally 'had a share (بهره داشت) of every (هر) knowledge (دانشِ)'; داشت is the past-tense lexical verb 'had' and governs the noun phrase 'بهره'.
segments.s585-g19: Poetic ordering: به پیش اندر آمدش = 'came into his presence' (به پیش اندر = 'into/before [him]'; آمدش = آمد + -ش enclitic 'to him' = 'came to him'). آهو دو جفت is 'two pairs of gazelles' (جفت after آهو for poetic emphasis). The verb (آمدش) takes the preceding locative phrase and the noun phrase as its subject.
segments.s585-g20: جوانمرد here is a substantive 'chivalrous man(s)' or 'noblemen'; خندان is a participial adjective 'smiling' following the noun. به + آزاده گفت is 'said to the noble one' — به is the dative preposition 'to', آزاده is the addressee (vocative/accusative), and گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here used elliptically for 'they said').
segments.s585-g21: که‌ای is an archaic/exclamatory vocative particle (که + ای) used to address someone: 'O ...'. ماه من = 'my moon' (a term of endearment). چون here is the interrogative/adverb 'how' introducing the question 'how shall I...'. کمان را is the definite object marker-ra attached to 'bow'. به زه = 'to/against the bowstring' (به as preposition + زه 'bowstring').
segments.s585-g22: برآرم is the 1st-person subjunctive/optative-form (archaic poetic) of برآوردن meaning 'I draw/raise' here 'shall I draw'. به شست = 'with the thumb' where به + instrumental sense ('by/with') governs شست 'thumb'. اندر آرم is 'I place/put in' (اندر = 'in/into', آرم = 'I put/may I put' — an archaic present/subjunctive form). گره is the object 'knot'. The sequence describes the manual action of drawing and setting the knot.
segments.s585-g23: کدام آهو is the interrogative 'which gazelle'. افگنده is a past-participle/adjectival verbal sense 'struck down / smitten' used with خواهی ('will you...') to form an outcomeative question (افگنده خواهی = 'will you have smitten/bring down'). به تیر = 'by/with an arrow' (prepositional instrument). The clause asks which of the animals he intends to shoot.
segments.s585-g24: که introduces a comment/explanation 'for/that': 'که ماده جوانست' = 'for the female is young'. ماده here means 'female (of an animal)'. جوانست is the copular clause 'is young' (جوان + است fused in poetic diction). و links the next clause: همتاش (همتا + -ش enclitic = 'its match/counterpart') پیر = 'is old'. This sets up the contrast between the young female and the old counterpart.
segments.s585-g1: جز از ... نبودیش کار is an idiomatic negating frame: جز از + X = “except for X”; نبودیش (literally “there was not to him”) functions as a negative existential/possessive idea meaning “he had no … (other)”. Here کار (“occupation/work”) is the head noun: “his occupation was nothing except …”. Word order is classical: exception phrase (جز از گوی و میدان) precedes the predicate (نبودیش کار).
segments.s585-g2: گهی ... و گاهی ... enumerates alternating occurrences: گهی/گاهی = “sometimes”. زخم چوگان is a genitive-like phrase “wound of the چوگان” (i.e. wound from the polo‑mallet). The verb is implied from the prior clause (it continues the description of his “work/experience”): “sometimes wounds from the mallet and sometimes from the hunt.”
segments.s585-g3: چنان ... که is a correlative “so … that / so that”: چنان here sets up the ensuing circumstance. بد is classical/adverbial here (not strong moral ‘bad’) and participates in the fixed formula چنان بد که = “so (it was) that …”. یک روز بی‌انجمن places the time and manner: “one day, without company.”
segments.s585-g4: به denotes direction/place: “to/into” (به نخچیرگه = “to the hunting‑ground”). رفت is simple past “he went.” با + (چنگ زن) marks accompaniment; چنگ زن here is a compound meaning “harp‑player” (چنگ = harp, and -زن is the agentive element). The whole segment: “he went to the hunting‑ground with a harp‑player.”
segments.s585-g5: کجا is an interrogative/exclamative “where/where then” used rhetorically. نام here means “name” in the sense of fame or renown; آن رومی آزاده = “that Roman (rûmī) noble/free‑hearted man.” بود is simple past “was.” The clause asks/reflects on the fame of that noble Roman.
segments.s585-g6: که is a relative pronoun “who/that.” رنگ = “color/complexion.” رخانش is the possessed noun (here referring to his face/complexion — a classical elided form) and is being qualified by رنگ; به می‌داده بود is past continuous “was giving/used to give” (می‌داده + بود = past habitual/continuous). Together: “who used to give (a) color to his (complexion)” — i.e. “whose complexion used to be radiant.”
segments.s585-g7: به پشت هیون = “upon the back of the beast”: به (to/on) + پشت (back, body‑part) + هیون (beast/animal). چمان is an adverbial/particle meaning “thus/so” or “in such a manner.” برنشست is past “he sat (upon).” Word order places the locative prepositional phrase before the verb, as is common in poetry.
segments.s585-g8: This is a compact descriptive tag: ابا سرو آزاده is a terse epithet—sرو (“cypress”) evokes tall/slender beauty, آزاده “noble/free‑hearted”; the initial ابا here functions as part of the descriptive phrase (an archaic/poetic element introducing the epithet). چنگی به دست = “a (harp) in hand” (چنگی refers to the harp/harp‑playing notion and به دست = “in hand”). The whole segment labels the rider as a noble, cypress‑like man holding a harp.
segments.s585-g25: بدو (to him) + گفت (said) is an inverted clause where the indirect object precedes the verb; آزاده is the speaker/agent ('the noble one') and کای is the vocative particle 'O' introducing شیرمرد ('lion‑hearted man' / 'champion'). In other words: 'The noble one said to him: "O, brave man..."'.
segments.s585-g26: به آهو is a prepositional phrase 'at/against a gazelle'; نجویند is a classical present form where the initial ن negates the verb (they do not seek) so نجویند = 'do not seek' (3rd plural); مردان (subject) follows the verb for emphasis and نبرد is the object 'battle' (they do not seek battle with a gazelle).
segments.s585-g27: تو is the 2nd‑person subject 'you'; آن ماده را is the definite object with را ('that female'); نر گردان is an imperative: نر (male) + گردان (imperative 'make/turn into') = 'make into a male'; به تیر is an instrumental/adverbial 'with/by an arrow.'
segments.s585-g28: شود is 3rd‑person singular of شدن 'become' (here future/resultative: 'will become'); ماده is the theme 'the female'; از تیر تو 'by/from your arrow' gives the cause/means; نر پیر is the predicate nominal 'an old male' (نر = male, پیر = old). Word order is poetic: predicate follows the cause and verb.
segments.s585-g29: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that/thereupon'; هیون را is the object 'the beast/animal' (را = definite object marker); برانگیز is an imperative 'rouse/drive up' (from برانگیختن); تیز functions adverbially 'swiftly/keenly.'
segments.s585-g30: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; آهو is the subject 'the gazelle'; ز چنگ تو is a prepositional phrase 'from your grasp' (ز = from, چنگ = clutch/hand/claw, تو = your); گیرد is 3rd‑person singular present 'takes' and here combines with the noun گریز 'flight' to mean 'takes to flight' or 'begins to flee.'
segments.s585-g31: کمان مهره انداز is a compound phrase: کمان 'bow' + مهره 'bead/pellet' + انداز (here part of the compound meaning 'that throws/shoots pellets') — together 'the bead‑(or pellet‑)shooting bow'. تا introduces purpose/result 'so that'; گوش خوی ش (split across خوی + ش) = 'his own ear' (خوی is the base and ش is the possessive clitic 'his').
segments.s585-g32: نهد is 3rd‑person singular of نهاد/نهادن here 'to place/put'; هم‌چنان 'thus/likewise' links resultively to the previous تا clause; خوار 'despised/ashamed' functions as a predicate adjective; بر دوش خوی ش = 'upon his own shoulder' (بر = on/upon, دوش = shoulder, خوی+ش = 'his own'). The clause means 'he will thus put/receive disgrace upon his own shoulder.'
segments.s585-g49: Structure: numeral + noun followed by two prepositional phrases. 'دو پیکان' = 'two arrows' (noun phrase). 'به جای' is a fixed prepositional expression meaning 'instead of'; it takes the following noun 'سرو' (cypress). 'در سرش' is a locative phrase 'in his head', with the possessive enclitic -ش attached to سر meaning 'his/its head'. Word order is typical Persian: modifiers and prepositional phrases follow the noun.
segments.s585-g50: 'به' here functions adverbially ('by/with' or 'with the agency of') introducing the manner/content — 'with blood'. 'اندرون' = 'inside/inner part(s)'. 'لعل گشته' uses گشته (the perfect/participial form of شدن) to mean 'has become/turned into' — so 'the inner part has become ruby'. 'برش' follows and is the noun meaning 'his cut/wound' or 'his slice' (here referring to the wound on him); the phrase as a whole says the inner parts, with blood, have turned ruby on his wound.
segments.s585-g51: 'هیون را' marks 'the beast' with the direct-object/ topical marker را — here the construction indicates the beast is the one driven/caused to act. 'سوی' = 'toward(s)' and 'جفت دیگر' = 'the other mate/pair'. 'بتاخت' is the past form of تاختن ('to gallop/charge'); the initial ب- is part of the classical past form here and the verb is third-person past 'charged'.
segments.s585-g52: The prepositional phrase 'به خم کمان' = 'into/on the bow's curve' (خم = curve, کمان = bow). 'مهره در مهره' is a reduplicative adverbial idiom meaning 'bead by bead' or 'one bead after another' (word-for-word 'bead in bead'). 'ساخت' is past tense 'made/constructed' — the implied subject (he) is omitted, typical in Persian. The whole clause describes making or setting beads along the bow's curve.
segments.s585-g53: 'به گوش' = 'into/onto the ear' (prepositional locative). 'یکی آهو' = 'a certain gazelle' (one of the gazelles; یکی here = 'a certain'/'one'). 'اندر' = 'in/into' (poetic variant of در). 'فکند' is the past of انداختن/فکندن meaning 'he put/placed/threw' — here 'he put (something) into the ear of a gazelle.' The omitted object (what was put) is recoverable from context but not repeated here.
segments.s585-g54: 'پسند آمد' is an idiomatic impersonal/periphrastic construction meaning 'it pleased' or 'it was pleasing'. 'آمد' is the simple past 'came' used idiomatically with پسند. 'و بود جای پسند' = 'and it was a place of liking' or 'and it was a desirable spot' — 'بود' is the past of 'to be', and جای پسند is a noun phrase 'place of liking/choice'. The clause states that the placement pleased and was appropriate/desirable.
segments.s585-g55: 'بخارید' is an intransitive past form meaning 'was pricked/twitch(ed)' (from خاریدن in this sense 'to be pricked'); here it describes a sudden movement of the ear. 'گوش آهو' is the subject 'the gazelle's ear' (possessive relation), and 'اندر زمان' = 'at that moment' (temporal phrase).
segments.s585-g56: Grammatically 'جادو کمان' is a genitive-like juxtaposition meaning 'the bow's magic/enchantment' (poetic inversion: noun + modifier). 'آورد' is the simple past 'brought/caused'. The construction 'به تیر اندر آورد' indicates movement 'caused to go into the arrow' or more naturally here 'caused an arrow to be driven in' — so the clause says that, by the bow's magic, an arrow was sent/forced inward. Word order is compact and somewhat inverted due to poetic register.
segments.s585-g41: هم‌انگه is a temporal adverb meaning 'at that moment' placed before the clause. چو introduces a simile 'like/as' and governs آهو ('gazelle'), while شد is a past tense verb 'became/took (to)' linking the subject (omitted or carried over from context) to the predicate. اندر گریز is a prepositional-phrase complement 'in flight/into flight' with اندر (in) + the noun گریز (flight). Word order is topical adverb → simile phrase → verb → locative phrase, typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s585-g42: This is a nominal phrase where سپهبد is the (agent/figure) noun 'spahbed/commander'. سروهای is a plural noun 'cypresses' used metaphorically (cypress-like features, e.g. graceful necks); آن is the demonstrative 'that' (marking possession/identification: 'that one's'); نره 'male' qualifies آن (i.e. 'that male'); and تیز is an adjectival attribute 'swift/keen' modifying نره or the whole noun phrase. The verb that governs this phrase is omitted here (a common poetic ellipsis) and appears in the surrounding lines.
segments.s585-g43: به تیر is an instrumental/agentive phrase 'by/with (the) arrow' (به used here in the 'by' sense). دو پیکان is the direct object 'two shafts/arrows'. ز سر is a prepositional phrase 'from the head' (ز = from + سر = head). برگرفت is a past-tense transitive verb 'took up / removed' (3rd singular). Word order places the instrumental and object before the verb, consistent with Persian poetic word order.
segments.s585-g44: کنیزک is the subject 'the little maid/maidservant'. بدو is a dative/clitic-like prepositional phrase 'at it / at that / to it' referring back to the prior action and functioning as the object of emotional reaction. ماند is the past verb 'remained/was left' (3rd singular). اندر شگفت is a locutive/state phrase 'in wonder/astonishment' (اندر = in, شگفت = wonder/astonishment). The whole means 'the maid remained astonished at it'; بدو marks the cause/target of her wonder.
segments.s585-g45: هم‌اندر زمان is a temporal phrase 'at the same time' or 'at that moment' (هم‌اندر = together/also + زمان = time). نر is the subject 'the male'; چون introduces the comparison/transition 'when/as' and governs ماده 'the female'. گشت is the past tense verb 'became/turned' (3rd singular). The clause reads 'at that time the male, when he became a female…' — گشت functions as a copular/transformative verb.
segments.s585-g46: سرش is 'his/its head' (سر + enclitic ش = his/her/its). زان is a pronominal/locative 'from that/thereby' or 'because of that' linking to the previous clause. سروی is adjectival/nominal 'cypress-like' (metaphorical description); سیه is the adjective 'dark/black'; ساده is 'plain/simple' — here describing appearance. گشت (again) is the past verb 'became/turned'. Grammatically this is a result clause: 'his head, because of that cypress-like [feature], became dark and plain.'
segments.s585-g47: همان is a deictic 'there/that very' (the same); در is the locative preposition 'in'. سروگاه is a compound 'cypress-place' (a poetic body-place or location associated with 'cypress', here likely the nape/neck area). ماده is the noun 'female' in genitive relation: 'the female's cypress-place'. دو تیر is the object 'two arrows'. This segment is a locative + object noun phrase preparing for the verb in the next segment.
segments.s585-g48: بزد is a past tense verb 'he struck/shot' (3rd singular). همچنان is an adverb 'likewise / in the same way'. مرد نخچیرگیر is an appositive/identifying noun phrase 'the hunter-man' (مرد = man/person; نخچیرگیر = hunter). The clause therefore reads 'the hunter likewise struck (shot)'. Note the verb follows the object phrase (دو تیر) as is common in these poetic lines.
segments.s585-g57: This is a noun list of body parts followed by a past verb: 'سر و گوش و پایِ-ش' (head and ear and his foot). The clitic ش is the third‑person possessive suffix attached to پای; به + پیکان here marks the instrument/agent ('by arrow(s)'); بدوخت is an archaic past form of دوختن used here in the sense 'was struck / pierced' (literally 'sewed' in other senses but used poetically for arrows hitting someone). Word order is normal: objects (body parts) then instrumental phrase then verb.
segments.s585-g58: بدان = به + آن ('to/for that') functioning as a demonstrative prepositional phrase modifying the following noun phrase. آهو آزاده را is the definite object ('that noble/fair gazelle') with را marking it as the referent of the emotion. دل بسوخت is an intransitive idiom ('the heart burned') meaning he felt sorrow/longing; بسوخت is past simple describing the state of the heart.
segments.s585-g59: بزد is simple past 'he struck'. دستِ بهرام is the direct object ('Bahram's hand' or 'the hand of Bahram'). و او را introduces a second object pronoun with را (emphatic/definite) 'and him'; ز + زین is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from the saddle' that will link to the verb in the next segment (the verb notionally applies to 'او را ز زین').
segments.s585-g60: نگونسار is an adjectival/adverbial qualifier 'woeful/ill‑fated/woebegone' modifying the ensuing action. برزد is an archaic past form (here meaning 'fell' or 'was cast down'); به روی زمین is the directional phrase 'onto the ground' (به = toward/into, روی = on/upon, زمین = ground). The verb completes the idea begun in the previous segment (he struck ... and (then) knocked him from the saddle, so he fell).
segments.s585-g61: هیون is the subject 'the beast/creature'. از بر is a poetic locative/ablative 'from beside/from the presence of' and takes ماه‌چهره ('the moon‑faced one / fair beauty') as the object of the action. براند is a third‑person past/archaic form of راندن 'to drive away'; the whole clause means 'the beast drove away the moon‑faced (one)'.
segments.s585-g62: برو here is a linking/temporal particle meaning 'thereupon/then' (archaic usage), introducing the next action. دست و چنگش is a coordinated noun phrase 'his hand and his claw' (چنگش = his claw/grip). به خون qualifies the manner/state 'in/with blood', and درفشاند is a past verb meaning 'he waved the standard/banner' (درفش = banner); together: 'then, with his hand and claw bloody, he waved the standard.'
segments.s585-g63: چنین گفت is a formulaic opening 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' — the printed sense list here is not the verb sense). کای is the vocative particle 'O', and بی‌خرد چنگ‌زن is the addressed noun phrase 'foolish harpist' (بی‌خرد = foolish, چنگ‌زن = harp‑player). The whole segment introduces direct speech.
segments.s585-g64: This is a rhetorical question: چه = 'what/why', بایست is a modal 'ought/should' (archaic), جستن is the infinitive 'to attempt/seek', به من marks the target 'against me / upon me', and برشکن is the verb 'to break' (here used as the action being attempted). The clause reads roughly 'Why should you try to break me?' — بایست + جستن forms a modal + infinitive construction.
segments.s585-g33: هم‌انگه is a temporal adverb ('at that moment') placed up front. ز + مهره here uses ز in a poetic instrumental sense 'with/from the bead' (i.e. using a bead as the instrument). بخاردش is the verb 'scratch' with the enclitic object -ش ('him/his/it') attached, so literal order is 'scratched-it' (he scratched his ear). گوش is the direct object 'ear'.
segments.s585-g34: بی‌آزار qualifies the action as 'gently/inoffensively'. پایش is 'his foot' (پای + enclitic -ش). برآرد is a past-tense classical form 'he lifted/brought up' (کردن compound sense 'bring/produce'), and به دوش uses به + دوش ('to/on the shoulder') where به has a directional/locative sense and دوش means 'shoulder'. The clause as a whole describes him placing or lifting his foot onto (or toward) his shoulder.
segments.s585-g35: به پیکان introduces the instrument/means ('with arrows' / 'onto arrows') using به. The sequence سر و پای و گوشش lists the targeted body parts 'head and feet and his ear'; the possessive enclitic -ش on the last noun governs the whole list. بدوز is an imperative/hortative form 'stitch/fasten/drive in' (here idiomatically meaning 'drive through / fix with') addressed to the addressee.
segments.s585-g36: چو here carries a conditional sense 'if'. خواهی is 2nd‑person present 'you want/wish'. که introduces the content clause that follows. خوانمت is 'I call/address you' (verb خواندن with the object enclitic -ت). گیتی فروز is a compound epithet 'Giti‑froz' literally 'world‑illuminating' — the addressee would be called this if the condition is met.
segments.s585-g37: کمان را uses the object marker را to mark 'the bow' as definite object. به زه کرد is a light‑verb construction: به زه 'to the bowstring' + کرد 'made/did' = 'strung' or 'drew (the) bowstring'. The proper name بهرام گور follows and functions as the agent (classical Persian allows this postverbal subject ordering): 'Bahram Gur (did this)'.
segments.s585-g38: برانگیخت is the past tense 'he aroused/raised'. از دشت آرام gives the source/location 'from the calm plain' (از = 'from/of', دشت = 'plain', آرام = 'calm/peaceful'). شور is the object 'tumult/commotion' (here the thing being raised). Word order is verb + source + object in this poetic line.
segments.s585-g39: دو پیکان is a nominative‑style phrase 'two arrows'. به ترکش is a locative phrase intended as 'in/at his quiver' (ترکش = 'quiver'); note that the provided lemma list separates forms, but in context ترکش functions as 'quiver'. یکی تیر داشت is a follow‑up 'he had one arrow' with داشت as the past‑tense 'had'. Overall the clause enumerates his arrows/munitions. (Because the provided sense list did not include 'quiver' for the TOKen labeled ترک, the sense index for that token is set to -1.)
segments.s585-g40: به دشت اندر is a locative phrase 'in/onto the plain' (اندر = 'in/within'). از بهر نخچیر داشت uses از بهر 'for the sake of' + نخچیر 'prey/game' and the verb داشت 'he had' — together meaning he possessed this arrow for hunting on the plain. The final داشت repeats the past possession/purpose idea.

sentences.s585-s5: She was his Dolaram and also his heart’s desire; you always kept her name on your lips.
sentences.s585-s6: On the day of the hunt you desired the beast, so that you might adorn its back with brocade.
sentences.s585-s7: From him were scattered four companions; you rode on, charging through heights and descents.
sentences.s585-s8: You outfitted his stirrups—two of gold, two of silver; to each one you gave a jewel-adorned (finish).
sentences.s585-s33: If you had been able to open it wide, I would have taken it away; because of this wound you have become a disgrace, my jewel.
sentences.s585-s34: When he cast him under the beast's foot into the fold, he did not take the little maid afterwards.
sentences.s585-s9: Right there, beneath his quiver, the bow bore a jewel; the hero possessed a share of every kind of knowledge.
sentences.s585-s10: Two pairs of gazelles came before him; the chivalrous men, smiling, spoke to the noble one.
sentences.s585-s11: “O my moon, how shall I draw the bow to the string, and with my thumb tie the knot?”
sentences.s585-s12: Which gazelle will you strike down with an arrow? The female is young while her match is old.
sentences.s585-s1: He had no occupation except the ball and the field; sometimes (he took) wounds from the polo-mallet and sometimes from the hunt.
sentences.s585-s2: So it was that one day, unaccompanied, he went to the hunting‑ground with a harp‑player.
sentences.s585-s3: Where then was the renown of that noble Roman, whose radiant complexion had shown itself?
sentences.s585-s4: He seated himself upon the beast’s back; the free, cypress‑tall (man) with a harp in his hand.
sentences.s585-s13: The nobleman said to him, 'O lion-hearted one, men do not seek battle with a gazelle.'
sentences.s585-s14: You, turn that female into a male with an arrow; the female, by your arrow, will become an old male.
sentences.s585-s15: After that, rouse the beast quickly; when the gazelle, from your grasp, takes flight.
sentences.s585-s16: Place the beaded (pellet‑shooting) bow to your ear, so that he will thereby bear disgrace on his own shoulder.
sentences.s585-s25: Two arrows were in his head instead of a cypress; the inner parts had turned ruby with blood on his wound.
sentences.s585-s26: The beast charged toward the other mate; on the bow's curve he set bead after bead.
sentences.s585-s27: He placed (it) into the ear of a certain gazelle; it pleased (her) and was a place of liking.
sentences.s585-s28: The gazelle's ear twitched at the moment; by the bow's enchantment an arrow was driven in.
sentences.s585-s21: At that moment, when it fled like a gazelle, the spahbed (commander/hunter) aimed at the cypress-like parts of that swift male.
sentences.s585-s22: By (his) arrow he took two shafts from the head; the little maid remained in wonder at it.
sentences.s585-s23: At the same time, when the male became (like) the female, his head, by that cypress-like feature, grew dark and plain.
sentences.s585-s24: There, in the female's cypress-place, two arrows he struck — the hunter did likewise.
sentences.s585-s29: His head and ears and foot were pierced by arrows; his heart burned for that noble gazelle.
sentences.s585-s30: He struck Bahram's hand and (then) from the saddle knocked him off; the ill‑fated man fell to the ground.
sentences.s585-s31: The beast drove away the moon‑faced (beauty), and then, with his hand and claw bloody, he waved the standard.
sentences.s585-s32: Thus he said, 'O foolish harpist, why do you attempt to break me?'.
sentences.s585-s17: At that moment he scratched his ear with a bead and, gently, lifted his foot onto his shoulder.
sentences.s585-s18: Pierce his head, feet, and ear with arrows — if you wish that I call you 'Giti‑froz' (World‑illuminator).
sentences.s585-s19: Bahram Gur strung the bow (drew the bowstring) and roused a commotion from the calm plain.
sentences.s585-s20: He had two arrows in his quiver and one arrow ready for hunting on the plain.
